tiers.label: Everyday home
tiers.desc: Practical, attainable finishes — the way most LA homes actually get remodeled.

tiers.label: Nice home
tiers.desc: Quality materials and considered design for well-kept homes in desirable neighborhoods.

archetypes.name: Craftsman / California Bungalow
neighborhoods: Long Beach (Rose Park, Bluff Heights, Wrigley)
signature: Low-pitched gabled roof with wide overhanging eaves, exposed rafter tails, and knee braces
Deep full-width front porch on tapered/battered wood columns set on brick, clinker-brick, or river-rock piers
Wood shingle or horizontal lap siding, often mixed for texture between stories
Multi-pane-over-single-pane double-hung windows (6-over-1, 4-over-1), sometimes grouped in threes
Brick or fieldstone porch piers and chimney
One-and-a-half-story profile with dormer; occasional airplane-bungalow third-story 'cockpit' massing
Small front yard set close to the sidewalk, narrow driveway to a detached rear garage (often ADU-converted)
interiorCharacter: Box-beam, boxed-beam, or coffered ceilings in living and dining rooms with picture-rail molding
Built-in bookcases, window seats, and a dining-room built-in hutch/buffet
Batchelder or similar art-tile fireplace surround, often with an inglenook
Quartersawn oak or Douglas fir trim, refinished doors and hardware
Wide cased opening between living and dining rooms; open living-to-dining sequence with minimal hallways
Narrow galley kitchen tucked behind the dining room
Leaded or art-glass panels in built-in cabinet doors; original oak or fir floors

typicalFinishes: Shaker or simple flat-panel cabinets echoing the original built-ins
butcher block or entry quartz counters
refinished original wood floors or matching LVP
period-style subway tile in the bath
original wood windows sometimes swapped for vinyl, painted (not stripped) original wood trim
everyday.renderGuidance: Honor the porch, exposed rafter tails, and built-ins as the identity of the house. Show real wear (worn floors, older appliances) but keep the box beams, built-ins, and window casings intact — this is a cared-for fixer, not a gutted shell. A sensible remodel updates the kitchen and bath modestly while keeping the narrow galley footprint and original room proportions.

typicalFinishes: Custom or semi-custom cabinetry matching original millwork (shaker/beadboard, glass-front)
quartz, soapstone, or natural stone counters
restored quarter-sawn oak or fir flooring and refinished built-ins
zellige, handmade, or reglazed Batchelder-style tile accents
restored wood windows with period hardware
vintage-look fixtures (schoolhouse pendants, glass doorknobs) with modern plumbing
nice.renderGuidance: Render a lovingly, sympathetically restored Craftsman — original woodwork, built-ins, inglenook, and porch detailing preserved and refinished, with a tasteful period-appropriate kitchen/bath update. Do not enlarge the kitchen beyond its original galley width or gut-modernize into a flat contemporary interior; it should read historic-and-cared-for.

typicalFinishes: Greene & Greene-inspired custom joinery
restored or hand-replicated Batchelder tile
museum-grade quartersawn oak restoration
designer landscape framing the porch
luxury.renderGuidance: Treat as a preservation showpiece: highlight hand-craft joinery and art tile as the star of the room; new elements (lighting, rugs, art) should recede, not compete.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve box-beam/coffered ceilings, built-in bookcases and inglenook, wood window casings, the dining-to-living cased opening, and any Batchelder tile fireplace; keep the kitchen a narrow galley behind the dining room rather than an open great room; change only paint, rugs, and furnishings — never strip original woodwork to white or add glossy modern finishes.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep the low-pitched gable roof, exposed rafter tails, tapered porch columns on brick/river-rock piers, and multi-pane wood windows intact; refresh paint, porch, and landscaping only.

avoid: Stripping or painting over quartersawn oak/fir trim white
Replacing multi-pane wood windows with plain vinyl sliders
Removing or enclosing the front porch or its tapered columns
Adding a second story or a flat modern roofline/parapet
Widening the kitchen into an open-concept great room or ripping out box beams and inglenooks
Swapping Batchelder tile for generic subway tile

sources.title: The Craftsman Homes of Pasadena | PBS SoCal
Bungalow Heaven, Pasadena, California — Wikipedia
Bungalow Heaven Home Tour | Bungalow Heaven Neighborhood Association
A Brief History Of The Iconic Bungalows Of L.A. — LAist
California bungalow — Wikipedia
HOUSE TOUR | A Los Angeles Craftsman Home Filled with Light & Charm | 4K
Tour the Back to the Future House — Inside the Craftsman Gamble House
House Tour: A 1947 California Craftsman Restored | Apartment Therapy
CHRISTMAS HOUSE TOUR | A 1920s Highland Park Home Transformed for the Holidays
Los Angeles Architecture 101: The Craftsman Home | The HartBeat

archetypes.name: Spanish Colonial Revival
signature: White or cream smooth-troweled stucco walls
Low-pitched clay barrel-tile roof (red-orange, variegated), sometimes just tile accents at the entry on modest examples
Arched doorways, windows, and covered entries as the decorative focal point
Wrought-iron window grilles, railings, and light fixtures
Asymmetrical facade massing, sometimes with a small tower or turret
Courtyard, patio, or loggia; cast-stone or decorative-tile entry surround
interiorCharacter: Arched interior doorways and niches
Coved or vaulted plaster ceilings
Decorative painted or Malibu/Talavera tile at stairs, kitchens, baths, and fountains
Exposed wood beam ceilings (stained, not painted) in living/dining rooms
Curved plaster staircase in two-story examples; wrought-iron stair railings and pendant fixtures
Saltillo terracotta or cement-tile flooring; deep-set windows with plaster reveals

typicalFinishes: Shaker or refaced cabinets in white or warm wood tone
quartz or butcher-block counters
Spanish/Saltillo-look LVP or refinished original wood floors
simple white subway or small-hex bath tile
original stucco with visible patch repairs, flat concrete 'S' tile substituted for true clay barrel tile on past reroofs
everyday.renderGuidance: Keep the arch, any coved ceiling, and the modest stucco-box massing even on a worn example; a believable everyday remodel modernizes the kitchen and bath but does not add a courtyard, fountain, hand-painted tile, or a second arch that wasn't in the source photo.

typicalFinishes: Custom cabinetry with a period-appropriate profile
natural stone, quartz, or soapstone counters with a tiled backsplash nod to the era
restored or reproduced Malibu/Catalina tile accents at fireplace and kitchen
refinished exposed wood beams and restored arches/plaster reveals
restored ironwork and wood doors
restored clay barrel-tile roof, warm neutral or off-white walls, terracotta or wood floors
nice.renderGuidance: Render an authentic period Spanish home tastefully updated, not a stripped-down modern remodel: new cabinetry and counters modernize while arches, beams, and tile accents stay as the room's structure. Stay within the home's one-story-or-modest footprint — a richer restoration can add authentic detailing (tile risers, iron sconces) but this is a family home, not a hacienda.

typicalFinishes: Hand-painted or reproduction imported tile from Mexico/Spain
custom wrought-iron ornamentation by period-specialist fabricators
carved wood doors
restored curved plaster staircases and coffered/stenciled ceilings
designer courtyard with fountain and mature specimen landscaping
luxury.renderGuidance: Render as a Wallace Neff-caliber estate: generous room proportions, a formal courtyard axis, and artisan tile/ironwork as focal details — never generic granite-and-recessed-can finishes. Use only when the brief calls for an estate-level Spanish Revival showpiece.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve arched openings, coved/vaulted plaster ceilings, exposed wood beams, and decorative Malibu/Talavera tile; keep wrought-iron fixtures and Saltillo/cement-tile floors and normal proportions rather than swapping in generic gray LVP or an open great room.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep the barrel clay-tile roof, white/cream stucco massing, arched windows and doors, and wrought-iron details; never flatten the roof pitch, square off the arches, or add a full clay-tile roof where the source shows only tile accents.

avoid: Flattening the barrel-tile roof into a flat or composition-shingle roof
Squaring off arched doorways/windows into rectangular openings
Painting stucco a saturated non-period color such as black or bright blue
Stripping decorative tile and wrought iron for a minimalist look
Adding a courtyard, fountain, or second story not present, or turning a modest starter home into a hacienda-scale estate
Replacing coved plaster ceilings with flat drywall and rows of recessed cans

sources.title: Hancock Park HPOZ | Los Angeles City Planning
What Is a Spanish-Style Home? History & Examples in Los Angeles
LOS ANGELES HOUSE TOUR | A Designer's Bright & Airy Spanish Revival Home
Inside LA's Most Stunning $7,000,000 Spanish Revival Estate (Eagle Rock)
HOUSE TOUR | Interior Designer Transforms Spanish Revival Home in Los Angeles
Houzz Tour: Honoring the Soul of a 1920s Spanish-Style Home
Dream House Tour: Beautiful Spanish Revival Home in Los Angeles
LA Home Styles: Modern, Mid-Century & Spanish

archetypes.name: Mid-Century Modern (Post-and-Beam / Eichler-adjacent)
signature: Post-and-beam construction with exposed structural beams read on the exterior facade
Low-slope or flat roof expressing the structural rhythm, with deep overhangs
Clerestory windows at the roofline
Floor-to-ceiling glass walls and sliding glass doors opening to a rear patio
Atrium or interior courtyard (Eichler atrium models)
Breeze-block or screen-block privacy walls; board-formed concrete, brick, or vertical wood facade
interiorCharacter: Exposed post-and-beam / tongue-and-groove wood-decking ceilings, often stained rather than painted
Open living/dining anchored by a linear brick or stone fireplace
Radiant-heated concrete slab, terrazzo, polished concrete, or wide-plank walnut/oak floors
Open kitchen-to-family-room plan with minimal interior walls
Flush-panel built-in cabinetry, minimal applied trim
Walls of glass blur indoor/outdoor; mahogany or painted-plywood accent walls

typicalFinishes: Original single-pane aluminum sliders
dated original kitchen (laminate counters, harvest-gold or avocado appliances)
unrestored radiant slab
worn but intact post-and-beam structure
everyday.renderGuidance: Show the original bones (beams, glass walls, atrium) unrestored and a bit dated — do not modernize the roofline or add ornament that erases the period character.

typicalFinishes: Walnut-stained or white-oak flat-panel cabinetry
quartz or honed stone counters in warm tones
restored or reproduction breeze-block screens
dual-pane glass in slim period-profile frames
refinished tongue-and-groove ceilings and original hardwood
period-appropriate brass or matte-black fixtures, globe/sputnik lighting
nice.renderGuidance: Warm mid-century, not showroom-modern: stained wood tones, textured stone, and era-correct fixtures rather than glossy white minimalism. New glazing and kitchen finishes read as an upgrade, but the post-and-beam rhythm, clerestories, and atrium stay the visual anchor. Keep the palette warm (walnut, brick, olive/mustard) over cold gray.

typicalFinishes: Book-matched wood paneling and custom walnut millwork
restored original steel-frame windows or structural glazing replacing sliders
polished concrete or terrazzo floors
integrated smart-home lighting hidden in period-correct fixtures
designer pool aligned to the post-and-beam grid
architect-grade landscaping (Marmol Radziner / Woods+Dangaran caliber)
luxury.renderGuidance: Treat as an A. Quincy Jones / Marmol Radziner-caliber restoration: material honesty and structural rhythm are the luxury, not applied ornament. Every choice reads intentional and expensive but true to the period — never a glass box. Suitable only for a hillside architectural-showpiece brief.

renderClauses.interior: Keep exposed post-and-beam structure, tongue-and-groove ceilings, and full-height glass to the yard/atrium visible; use warm wood tones (walnut/oak), a linear stone or brick fireplace, and period-correct lighting; furnish with authentic mid-century pieces mixed with comfortable modern seating, not ornate traditional furniture or sterile staging.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the low horizontal volume, low/flat roofline, deep overhangs, post-and-beam rhythm, clerestory windows, and glass walls; add textural landscaping (agave, olive, gravel); do not add pitched roofs, shutters, or traditional trim.

avoid: Adding a pitched shingle roof, dormers, or a second story that breaks the low horizontal roofline
Boxing in the post-and-beam structure with drywall
Replacing glass walls with smaller punched windows
Tuscan/Spanish detailing, crown molding, chandeliers, or wrought iron mixed into an MCM shell
Enclosing the atrium/courtyard
All-white glossy minimalism (that reads contemporary, not mid-century)

sources.title: Balboa Highlands — LA Conservancy
The Eichlers of Balboa Highlands - Granada Hills | Eichler Network
Architect Restores a Mid-Century Modern House In The Hills of Los Angeles (House Tour)
19 Laudable Midcentury Modern Renovations in Los Angeles
This mid century gem's clever remodel maximizes outdoor space (Studio City)
Why Crestwood Hills Is a Haven for Mid-Century Modern Enthusiasts | O'Connor Estates
Case Study House #10 — LA Conservancy

archetypes.name: Post-war California Ranch
signature: Low-pitch gable or hip roof with modest-to-wide eave overhang and horizontal massing (wider-than-tall street face)
Attached 1-2 car garage flush with or forward of the facade, concrete driveway apron
Frame-and-stucco or board-and-batten / brick-and-stucco exterior mix on a small rectangular lot
Picture window (sometimes flanked by shutters or a decorative screen-block wall) facing the street
Simple covered entry stoop rather than a full porch
Private backyard patio slab, often with a covered aluminum or wood patio cover
interiorCharacter: 7.5-9 ft ceilings; compact entry opening directly into the living room
Galley or U-shaped kitchen with a pass-through to a small dining area (often expanded into a former nook or service porch)
Hall-and-bedroom-wing layout, 2-3 modest bedrooms off one hallway
Original oak strip or slab flooring, frequently carpeted over by later owners
Sliding glass or French door to a modest back patio
Opened-up living/dining/kitchen and primary-suite additions common in remodeled examples

typicalFinishes: Stock or semi-custom shaker cabinets in white or gray
laminate or entry-level quartz countertop
LVP flooring through the main living areas
standard subway-tile tub surround
builder-grade stainless appliances
everyday.renderGuidance: Style the interior as a tidy, sensible refresh of a real ranch: keep the modest room sizes and 8-ft ceilings, use approachable finishes (white shaker, LVP, entry quartz) rather than designer materials, and leave the original footprint and window placements alone.

typicalFinishes: Painted or two-tone wood shaker cabinets
quartz or quartzite counters
wide-plank white-oak or oak-look engineered flooring
subway or handmade-look zellige backsplash
framed glass shower enclosure
matte-black or brushed-brass fixtures, recessed LED plus a statement island pendant
resurfaced patio, drought-tolerant front yard
nice.renderGuidance: A step up but still a single-story ranch that reads lived-in and family-owned after a smart, tasteful remodel — quality mid-range materials, warm and a little imperfect, never a staged estate. Keep the modest square footage and 8-9 ft ceilings (no vaulting unless the room is a known vaulted addition), simple massing, and no great-room-scale islands or anything reading above ~$2M.

renderClauses.interior: Respect the original 8-9 ft ceiling height and the compact hall-and-bedroom-wing layout; render a galley or modest U-shaped kitchen with real furniture and a lived-in feel (not an open-concept great room or showroom staging) unless the source photo already shows a wall removed.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep the low-slung one-story horizontal massing, wide eaves, and existing roofline; show upgrades as refreshed stucco/board-and-batten paint, a new garage door, a refreshed front door, and simple drought-tolerant or lawn-plus-hedge landscaping rather than any change to the building's shape or height.

avoid: adding a second story that breaks the ranch's horizontal silhouette
vaulting or raising ceilings
widening the house footprint
turning the picture window into floor-to-ceiling glass or a glass-walled great room
rendering a 10-ft kitchen island in a galley-width kitchen
marble waterfall islands, brass-everything, resort pools, grand double-door entries or two-story foyers (reads luxury, not this tier)

sources.title: LOS ANGELES HOUSE TOUR | A 1950s Ranch Home Gets a Stunning Makeover
Tour a 1950s ranch home renovated for a family of three
The Art of Restoring a 1950s Los Angeles Home | House Tour
The Lakewood Plan: Homeownership, Taxes, and Diversity in Postwar Suburbia
Sherman Oaks Real Estate in 2026: Why Buyers Are Competing Hard for a Shrinking Inventory
2026 Los Angeles Kitchen Remodel Costs and Tips
Bathroom Remodel Cost Los Angeles (2026 Pricing Guide)

archetypes.name: Tudor / Storybook & Period-Revival Traditional
signature: Steeply pitched cross-gable roofs (Tudor); storybook examples add a rolled 'catslide' eave; Colonial examples a symmetrical hip roof; Minimal Traditional a simplified low-eave hip/gable
Decorative half-timbering on upper gables; brick or clinker-brick veneer with a prominent chimney (or, on Minimal Traditional, plain stucco/lap siding with little ornament)
Tall narrow windows, often diamond-pane leaded glass grouped in threes; multi-pane divided-light windows with shutters on Colonial examples
Round-arched or paneled wood entry door recessed under a small hood or portico
Detached garage set back from the main house
interiorCharacter: Formal entry hall separating living and dining rooms in larger examples; Minimal Traditional keeps small efficient rooms
Brick fireplace with an arched firebox and a tile or carved-wood mantel as the living-room focal point
Exposed (often decorative) half-timber beams; leaded or diamond-pane windows
Curved walnut or oak staircase with turned newel post in two-story examples
Coved or simple crown molding, taller baseboards than a ranch; plaster walls, wood-plank or herringbone floors
Built-in china cabinet between the dining room and a modernized rear kitchen

typicalFinishes: Original modest Minimal Traditional cottages or small Tudors with dated kitchens
stock shaker or refaced cabinets
laminate or butcher-block counters
sheet vinyl or LVP flooring
single-pane windows and worn original flooring
everyday.renderGuidance: Keep the small, efficient room scale of a working-class 1940s cottage honest — a real remodel is fresh paint, budget or refaced cabinets, and simple flooring. Don't inflate it into an open-concept great room or add a porch/portico that wasn't original.

typicalFinishes: Refinished original oak or walnut flooring; restored leaded/diamond-pane glass
restored or reproduced coved molding and picture rails
kitchen updated with shaker or inset cabinetry, quartz or marble-look counters, keeping brick/arch motifs
restored original wood windows or matching divided-light replacements
period-appropriate brass or oil-rubbed-bronze hardware and lighting
nice.renderGuidance: Render a well-kept period traditional with its formal bones — entry hall, fireplace mantel, staircase, brick, and leaded windows — preserved, with kitchen and baths tastefully modernized. It should read as a solid family home in an established neighborhood, warm and formal-but-lived-in, not museum-restored or over-scaled into a great room.

typicalFinishes: Custom millwork and restored half-timbering
imported or replica leaded glass
formal Windsor Square-caliber Tudor estate finishes
luxury.renderGuidance: Render as a formal Windsor Square-style estate: generous, symmetrical rooms with restored half-timber and leaded glass as centerpiece details.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve brick fireplaces with arched fireboxes, leaded/diamond-pane windows, exposed half-timber beams, the formal entry hall and any turned-newel staircase, and coved/crown molding; keep rooms modestly scaled for Minimal Traditional and storybook examples rather than inflating them into open-concept great rooms.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep the steep cross-gable or catslide roofline, half-timbering, and brick veneer (Tudor/Storybook) — or the simple low-ornament hip/gable massing (Minimal Traditional) — and the round-arched entry intact; do not flatten the roof, strip half-timber detailing, or add Spanish stucco-and-tile details onto a Tudor frame.

avoid: Removing half-timbering or brick veneer for a smooth modern facade
Replacing diamond-pane leaded windows with plain large panes
Flattening a steep catslide/cross-gable roof or removing the chimney
Over-scaling a Minimal Traditional cottage into a McMansion great room
Stripping the round-arched entry door for a flush modern door
Adding a porch/portico or picture windows that weren't original

sources.title: LOS ANGELES CITYWIDE HISTORIC CONTEXT STATEMENT — Period Revival 1919-1950 | LA City Planning
Minimal Traditional (1935-1950), City of Whittier Historic Preservation
Minimal Traditional — Wikipedia
Minimal Traditional | Fullerton Heritage
Step Inside This MAGICAL Fairytale Tudor Cottage (1928 Storybook Tudor)
Exploring Tudor-Style Homes in Los Angeles: Architecture, History & Neighborhoods
Bixby Knolls: Long Beach's Hidden Gem | Neighborhood Tour
Discover Historic Bixby Knolls in Long Beach Today

archetypes.name: Modest Stucco Box
signature: Flat or very low-pitch roof with minimal or no eave overhang
Plain rectangular stucco box massing, almost no ornament
Small aluminum-frame windows, often slider style
Attached single-car garage taking up much of the street facade
Narrow concrete walkway to a flush, unadorned front door
Chain-link or block-wall side-yard fencing; small flat concrete stoop instead of a porch
interiorCharacter: 7.5-8 ft ceilings
Very simple rectangular room layout with minimal hallway
Small original kitchen, often galley or one-wall
Combined living/dining space in smaller examples
1 bath, 2-3 bedrooms; original slab floors under vinyl tile, sheet vinyl, or worn carpet
Small single-pane aluminum windows letting in less light than earlier styles

typicalFinishes: Stock shaker cabinets or cabinet refacing
laminate or entry-level quartz counters
LVP or sheet vinyl flooring
fiberglass shower-tub insert or basic tile surround
vinyl replacement windows
everyday.renderGuidance: This is the plainest, most budget-driven housing type in the set — render finishes as clean and functional, not stylish; the goal is 'a family fixed it up on a real budget,' with builder-grade materials and no architectural flourishes added.

renderClauses.interior: Keep ceilings at a plain 7.5-8 ft and rooms boxy and small; do not introduce vaulted ceilings, exposed beams, or a large island that wouldn't fit the original one-wall or galley kitchen.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep the flat/low-pitch roof and unadorned box massing exactly as photographed; treat upgrades only as paint, a new garage door, window replacement, and landscaping.

avoid: adding decorative roof pitch, gables, or eaves that weren't there
vaulting ceilings
adding a porch
rendering designer/luxury finishes out of step with a budget stucco box
changing the window style from slider/aluminum to large picture windows

sources.title: Tract Housing in California, 1945-1973 (LA City Planning historic context report)
The Art of Restoring a 1950s Los Angeles Home | House Tour
Why 1940s Los Angeles Houses Are Built to Last

archetypes.name: Suburban Tract Two-Story
signature: Two-story massing with a repeated floor plan shared by neighboring houses
Attached 2-3 car garage (sometimes side-loaded/tandem) as the dominant street-facing element
Stucco field (warm tan/beige) with brick or wood-look trim accents
Mediterranean-influenced concrete-tile or composition-shingle roof, often a prominent front-facing gable
Two-story entry portico with sidelight windows flanking the front door
Modest HOA-consistent front yard; backyard sized for a pool/patio
interiorCharacter: 8-ft ceilings on both floors with a two-story entry foyer, sometimes a curved or straight staircase visible from the front door
Formal living/dining rooms flanking the entry (often with arched doorway openings), family room open to the kitchen at the rear
Kitchen with an eat-in nook plus peninsula or a raised-bar island (era-dependent)
Primary suite upstairs with the other bedrooms; loft/bonus room overlooking the entry in 90s-2000s plans
Builder-grade oak trim and six-panel doors; original wall-to-wall carpet upstairs
Interior laundry/mud area off the garage

typicalFinishes: Stock or semi-custom shaker cabinets
laminate or entry quartz counters
LVP downstairs, refreshed carpet upstairs
standard tub-shower combo with tile surround
stock stainless appliances
everyday.renderGuidance: Render as a typical suburban production-builder home refreshed on a normal budget: keep the two-story tract footprint and repeated-plan proportions, and use everyday materials rather than custom builder finishes.

typicalFinishes: Painted or transitional semi-custom cabinets
quartz counters with a modest waterfall or eased edge
engineered hardwood or tile on the main floor
framed glass shower with tile to ceiling
refreshed staircase (iron balusters), updated primary bath with freestanding tub or larger shower
repainted stucco, new garage doors, updated front door and lighting
nice.renderGuidance: A nicer finish-out of the same production floor plan — better materials and a cleaner palette — but the two-story massing, roofline, two-story foyer, and formal/casual room split stay identical to the original tract plan. Family-lived-in scale, not a custom estate; avoid infinity pools or resort landscaping.

renderClauses.interior: Keep the builder floor plan intact — two-story entry foyer, formal front rooms with arched openings, family room/kitchen toward the back, bedrooms upstairs — and use an eat-in nook plus peninsula or a modest island (not a great-room gut) unless the source photo already shows one.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the two-story tract massing, garage-forward facade, prominent front gable, and concrete-tile or shingle roofline shared with neighboring houses; render upgrades as fresh stucco paint, garage door, entry door, and tidy refreshed landscaping only.

avoid: converting to single story, an open-concept great room, or adding a third story
changing the roof material or pitch, or flat modern rooflines
removing the two-story entry volume
making the house look custom/architect-designed rather than production-built
ultra-luxury resort backyards (swim-up bars, glass-edge pools) at this price point

sources.title: Explore the Rich History of Valencia in Santa Clarita Valley
Dawn of a new century: How Santa Clarita got here
Tour Santa Clarita Valley: Explore One of the Most Desirable Suburbs Near Los Angeles
Stevenson Ranch Homes for Sale | Stevenson Ranch, CA Real Estate
New Home Community in Porter Ranch by Toll Brothers
Single and One Story Homes in Porter Ranch, Los Angeles, CA For Sale | Redfin

archetypes.name: Hillside / Stilt Home
archetypes.eras: 1955-1965 (peak spec-built stilt era); custom hillside builds continuing to present
signature: Cantilevered decks projecting past the building line toward the view
Exposed steel or wood stilt/pole supports, often braced with diagonal cable or rod X-bracing
View-oriented glass wall on the downhill elevation
Minimal street presence — often only a garage/entry door visible from the road
Floating/cantilevered massing that steps down the slope
interiorCharacter: Open living rooms cantilevered toward the view with floor-to-ceiling glass
Exposed structural steel beams and columns integrated into the room
Narrow floor plates dictated by the buildable envelope
Split-level interior stairs following the hillside grade
Low-profile or glass guardrails preserving sightlines

typicalFinishes: Original 1958-65 spec-built stilt construction
small floor plates
dated finishes
may or may not have had a post-Northridge structural retrofit
everyday.renderGuidance: Note the structural constraint explicitly: this is a small, view-driven box on exposed supports, not a house that can be freely enlarged.

typicalFinishes: Retrofitted steel moment-frame bracing
updated glass walls and decking
engineered wood decking
modern glass or cable railings
nice.renderGuidance: A tasteful update keeps the deck footprint and stilt/brace geometry identical; only surface materials change.

typicalFinishes: Architect-grade cantilevered decks
structural glass walls
infinity-edge pool or deck elements
exposed high-end steel construction
luxury.renderGuidance: Reference Lautner/Koenig-era design language (e.g., Garcia House, Stahl House): the structure itself — visible steel, glass, and the drop into the canyon — is the luxury statement.

renderClauses.interior: Respect the narrow, view-oriented floor plate and exposed structural beams/columns; do not widen rooms or remove load-bearing posts/braces visible in the source photo — hillside structures cannot be freely reconfigured.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep cantilevered decks, stilt/pole supports, and any visible diagonal cable/rod bracing intact and structurally plausible; do not fill in the space below the house or add ground-level additions that ignore the slope.

avoid: Removing or 'hiding' visible stilts/diagonal bracing — they are load-bearing
Widening a cantilevered room footprint beyond the existing deck edge
Adding a full basement or ground-level garage under the house on a steep slope where none exists
Flattening or regrading the hillside in the render
Ignoring the view orientation — furniture and glazing should face the canyon/city, not the street
Adding heavy stone veneer that implies structural load the cantilever wasn't designed to carry

sources.title: Stilt Homes in Los Angeles: Modern Marvels with Iconic Hillside Views
Stilt Home Foundation Repair Los Angeles | Alpha Structural, Inc
Side-by-Side: Two Mid-Century Hillside Homes in LA With Completely Different Energy | House Tour
The Art of the Slope: Hillside Homes in Los Angeles | Saban Global

archetypes.name: Contemporary Small-Lot Infill / SB9-Era Townhome
signature: Zero or minimal setback from the property line
Vertical massing stacked 2-3 stories on a narrow lot
Flat or shed roofline, simple rectilinear volumes
Stucco, fiber-cement panel, or mixed concrete/glass/wood cladding
Small or no private yard; shared driveway or motor court serving a cluster of units
Rooftop deck in lieu of a yard
interiorCharacter: Open-plan living typically stacked on the upper floor for light and view
Bedrooms on a lower level, following the vertical stack
Large sliding glass to a small deck, patio, or Juliet balcony
Minimal applied trim; flat-panel cabinetry
Engineered wood or vinyl plank flooring for durability/cost

typicalFinishes: Builder-grade quartz or laminate counters
vinyl plank flooring
stock flat-panel cabinets
minimal trim, track lighting
everyday.renderGuidance: Keep it honest builder-modern spec finish — clean but not designer; don't add custom millwork this tier wouldn't have.

typicalFinishes: Upgraded quartz and engineered wood flooring
semi-custom cabinetry
glass deck railings
designer lighting on the rooftop deck
nice.renderGuidance: A modest upgrade in materials only — the compact stacked floor plan and small-lot massing stay fixed.

renderClauses.interior: Keep the compact, vertically-stacked floor plan and large sliding glass to the small deck/patio; use clean flat-panel cabinetry and minimal trim consistent with a builder-modern spec finish, not ornate traditional details.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the zero/minimal-setback massing, flat or shed roofline, and simple stucco/panel cladding; do not add wide setbacks, pitched roofs, or a full yard that doesn't exist on the lot.

avoid: Adding a large front or side yard where the lot has none
Turning a flat/shed roof into a pitched traditional roof
Adding ornate period trim (Craftsman/Spanish details) that doesn't match the builder-modern shell
Widening room footprints beyond the small-lot floor plate
Ignoring the shared driveway/motor-court context of the cluster development

sources.title: Starter home concepts seek to alleviate Los Angeles housing crisis — Dezeen
Small Lot Subdivision Los Angeles Architects | Modative
Small Lot Design Standards: Guide for Los Angeles Subdivisions | JDJ Consulting
Inside a Hidden House in Los Angeles Designed as a Private World (House Tour)

archetypes.name: Valley / Westside Condo & Townhome Interior
signature: Low-rise 2-3 story stucco building, usually with a shared pool or courtyard
Carport or subterranean shared parking
Small private balcony or patio slider per unit
Uniform HOA-governed facade, windows, and railings across the building
interiorCharacter: Popcorn (acoustic) ceilings, common through the late 1980s
Oak or builder-grade laminate cabinetry, often with brass hardware
Mirrored wardrobe/closet doors
Narrow galley kitchens
Carpet throughout, driven by shared-wall/floor acoustic requirements
Single sliding door to a small balcony or patio as the only 'outdoor' space

typicalFinishes: Original popcorn ceilings
oak cabinets
brass hardware and fixtures
wall-to-wall carpet
single-pane sliders
everyday.renderGuidance: Keep the unit's modest, boxy proportions and shared-wall context visible; this is a rental-adjacent unit, not a house — don't add windows or a yard that can't exist.

typicalFinishes: Popcorn ceiling removed/skim-coated
quartz counters
HOA-approved LVP or engineered wood (for sound rating)
matte-black or brushed-nickel hardware replacing brass
refreshed slider/balcony
nice.renderGuidance: A cosmetic-only upgrade within the existing envelope — walls, window openings, and balcony placement stay exactly where they are.

renderClauses.interior: Work within the existing modest room proportions and shared-wall layout; keep balcony/slider placement and any load-bearing walls between units — condo layouts can't be freely opened up like a single-family house.
renderClauses.exterior: Any exterior render must respect the shared building envelope and HOA-uniform facade — don't redesign the whole building's exterior to suit a single unit's remodel.

avoid: Removing walls that are shared with neighboring units
Redesigning the whole building's facade or balcony style for one unit's remodel
Ignoring typical HOA constraints (uniform windows, paint, balcony rails)
Adding a fireplace, extra windows, or a footprint expansion that isn't physically possible in a mid-building unit
Assuming a full yard exists when the unit only has a small patio or balcony

sources.title: An 80s-Style Condo Gets a Modern Makeover — Mountain Living
When you've lost your taste for popcorn ceilings — Tucson.com
Amazing Condo Makeover! Unbelievable Transformation Tour - Before and After Renovation
Tour a Stunning Los Angeles Condo's Bold Renovation | HGTV

archetypes.name: ADU / Garage Conversion
neighborhoods: Citywide — Van Nuys, North Hollywood, Reseda (Valley)
signature: Detached single-story box, or a converted attached garage footprint
Above-garage option adds a full second story over existing parking
Roofline and siding typically matched or simplified from the main house
Small footprint, often built to minimal rear/side setback
Small entry deck or patio in lieu of a formal entrance
interiorCharacter: Open-plan studio or one-bedroom layout
Compact galley or single-wall kitchen
Stacked or closet washer/dryer
Visible wall-mounted mini-split HVAC unit (no central ducting)
9-10 ft ceilings when new-construction (vs. lower in a raw garage conversion)
Pocket doors and built-in storage to conserve floor area

typicalFinishes: Basic garage-slab conversion
vinyl plank flooring
stock cabinets
minimal window openings
single mini-split
everyday.renderGuidance: Keep the footprint honestly garage-sized; finishes are basic and builder-grade, not designer.

typicalFinishes: New dual-pane windows
quartz counters
tiled shower
engineered wood flooring
properly sized mini-split
nice.renderGuidance: A well-finished detached or above-garage unit — still compact, but with intentional material choices.

typicalFinishes: Architect-designed detached ADU
full-height glass
radiant floors
high-end integrated appliances
luxury.renderGuidance: Treat as a mini architectural statement in the backyard — high design intent within a strictly small footprint.

renderClauses.interior: Respect the small compact footprint — keep the open-plan studio/1-bed layout, stacked laundry, and mini-split wall unit; don't render rooms larger than a garage-footprint ADU could hold.
renderClauses.exterior: Match or simplify the main house's roofline and siding on the ADU shell; keep the small footprint and setback realistic for a backyard structure, not a full second house.

avoid: Rendering an ADU with a footprint larger than a converted garage or small backyard structure can hold
Adding a full basement or a second full-size garage under the ADU
Ignoring the mini-split/wall-unit HVAC common to ADUs by implying central ducted systems that don't exist
Making the ADU exterior wildly mismatched in style from the main house without a stated design reason
Adding grand entries/porticos disproportionate to a small backyard unit

sources.title: Garage Conversion ADU Guide: Costs, Plans & Process in LA (2026) | CALI ADU
Above Garage ADU in Los Angeles: A Flexible Space Designed for Work, Guests & Entertaining
Los Angeles ADU tour. Before and after. 750 sq ft. $350K.
Los Angeles Above Garage ADU Tour | 500 Sq Ft of Smart Design

archetypes.name: Beverly Hills / Bel Air / Holmby Hills Classic Estate
archetypes.eras: 1920s-1940s Golden Age revival; 1980s-2000s spec-built revival; gut-renovated legacy estate
neighborhoods: Bel Air (Old Bel Air + Lower Bel Air)
signature: Gated motor court with fountain and formal porte-cochere
Symmetrical facade in Mediterranean, French Normandy, Georgian Colonial, or Tudor idiom
Clay tile, slate, or copper roofing
Formal foyer with sweeping curved staircase
Walled/hedged grounds with mature specimen trees
Guest house or staff quarters, tennis court, motor-court garage row (4-8 cars)
Resort-style rear grounds: pool, pool house, cabana, formal gardens
interiorCharacter: 12-16 ft formal ceilings on the main level, coffered or hand-plastered
Grand entry foyer with statement staircase and chandelier
Separate formal living room, formal dining room, and family/great room
Library/study with built-in millwork paneling
Wine cellar or wine room, home theater, gym, spa
Primary suite as a private wing with dual closets and sitting room
Service areas: butler's pantry, catering kitchen, staff hallway

typicalFinishes: Book-matched marble or onyx slab on fireplace walls and primary baths
hand-scraped or rift-sawn white oak wide-plank flooring, herringbone in formal rooms
custom fluted or paneled millwork, plaster crown detail
La Cantina or Fleetwood pocket doors bridging to loggias/terraces
panel-ready Sub-Zero/Wolf/Miele suites, dual islands, scullery kitchen
designer plumbing (Waterworks, Kallista, THG) and custom brass/bronze hardware
limestone or reclaimed antique stone exterior cladding, wrought-iron detailing
luxury.renderGuidance: Render with formal symmetry intact and generous ceiling height read through window proportions; keep stone/marble book-matching visible and continuous across slabs; grounds should read manicured and mature, not new-build landscaping; preserve grandeur — avoid flattening the estate into a generic McMansion.

renderClauses.interior: Maintain formal proportions and ceiling height; preserve existing architectural millwork, coffered ceilings, and staircase geometry; apply book-matched natural stone and rift-sawn white oak consistent with an Old Bel Air / Beverly Hills flats estate; do not introduce builder-grade finishes or shrink room scale.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the gated, walled estate silhouette, roofline material (clay tile/slate/copper), and symmetrical facade; render mature hedging and specimen trees, not new sod; keep motor court and porte-cochere proportions intact.

avoid: Shrinking 12+ ft ceilings to standard 9 ft in renders
Rendering builder-grade stock cabinetry or laminate counters into an estate kitchen
Stripping the formal symmetry of a Georgian/French facade into an asymmetrical modern box
Replacing mature landscaping with sparse new-build sod

sources.title: Touring a $64,000,000 Mansion in Beverly Hills
Historic 1918 Huntington-adjacent Georgian Colonial estate context (Myron Hunt, Colonial Revival)
Enes Yilmazer $68M Bel Air Mansion Tour
Enes Yilmazer YouTube channel (Bel Air/Beverly Hills estate tours)

archetypes.name: Hidden Hills / Calabasas Modern Farmhouse
signature: Board-and-batten and white/black vertical siding with black-framed windows
Gabled roofline, standing-seam metal roof accents, exposed wood beam entries
Oversized black steel-and-glass pivot front door
Circular gravel or paver motor court behind gates, often with a barn-style garage or detached guest barn
Resort backyard: pool/spa, outdoor kitchen, bocce/sport court, sometimes a bowling alley or indoor-outdoor pool sanctuary
Equestrian or flat usable lot (Hidden Hills) vs. view-lot in the Calabasas hills
interiorCharacter: 10-14 ft ceilings with exposed wood beams in the great room
Great-room concept: kitchen-dining-family room under one volume, disappearing glass walls to the yard
Shiplap or venetian-plaster accent walls
Oversized kitchen island (waterfall or furniture-style leg), scullery/prep kitchen behind the main kitchen
Wide-plank white oak flooring throughout
Black steel-frame interior doors and windows as a recurring motif
Primary suite wing with freestanding tub, walk-through shower, dual closets

typicalFinishes: Book-matched quartzite or marble waterfall islands
white oak wide-plank floors (rift-sawn, wire-brushed, or European oak)
La Cantina/Fleetwood multi-slide glass walls opening the great room to the yard
panel-ready Sub-Zero/Wolf/Thermador suites, hidden scullery
black steel windows/doors and matte-black plumbing fixtures
shiplap, venetian plaster, or limewash accent walls
wide driveway pavers, board-formed concrete or limestone exterior accents
luxury.renderGuidance: Render the great-room volume with visible wood beams and black steel glazing intact; keep the farmhouse-modern material palette (white/black exterior, warm wood, black steel) consistent between interior and exterior shots; grounds should read resort-new (crisp hedging, new hardscape) rather than legacy-estate mature, since this is new construction.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve the great-room volume, exposed beam ceiling, and black steel window/door grid; keep white oak flooring and waterfall-island proportions; maintain the farmhouse-modern palette of white/black/warm wood rather than drifting into pure minimalism or ornate traditional detailing.
renderClauses.exterior: Keep board-and-batten or vertical siding, black window frames, gabled massing, and the pivot door as the anchor feature; preserve gated motor-court scale and resort backyard layout (pool, outdoor kitchen); do not convert the massing into a flat-roof contemporary box.

avoid: Converting the gabled farmhouse roofline into a flat modern roof
Replacing black steel windows/doors with white vinyl
Shrinking the great-room ceiling or removing the exposed beams
Rendering this as a legacy/old-money estate — it should read new-build crisp

sources.title: $39,995,000 Modern Farmhouse in Hidden Hills 20,000 Sq Ft NEW Build with Indoor Pool & Spa Sanctuary
Inside a $40,000,000 Modern Farmhouse Mansion in Hidden Hills (Full Tour)
Inside a $40M Los Angeles Modern Farmhouse With a Bowling Alley!
Tour an Airy Farmhouse-Style Estate in Hidden Hills, California | Open House TV

archetypes.name: Hollywood Hills / Bird Streets / Sunset Strip Contemporary Hillside
archetypes.eras: 2010s-2026 architectural spec builds; midcentury remodels (Kappe/Lautner lineage)
signature: Cantilevered massing hugging a steep hillside lot with city/canyon/ocean views
Floor-to-ceiling glass curtain walls, often a two-story great room
Flat or low-slope roofline, board-formed concrete or Turkish travertine cladding
Linear infinity-edge pool cantilevered toward the view
Motor court behind a discreet gate at the end of a private street
Central atrium or courtyard with a mature specimen tree (jacaranda, olive)
interiorCharacter: Double-height great room combining living, kitchen, and dining under glass
Disappearing glass walls/pocket doors erasing the indoor-outdoor line
Monastic natural-material palette: stone slab walls, rift-sawn white oak, blackened steel
Primary suite as a private pavilion with its own terrace and soaking tub
Home theater, wine cellar, gym/spa, and often a second full kitchen on a lower entertaining level
Linear fireplace and minimal-frame glazing throughout

typicalFinishes: Fire-resilient Turkish travertine or limestone slab wall cladding
rift-sawn white oak flooring and cabinetry
minimal-frame or frameless glass walls (Fleetwood/Panoramah-level systems)
book-matched stone kitchen islands, panel-ready appliance suites, hidden scullery
linear/ribbon infinity pools with glass tile or dark plaster finish
integrated smart-home and wellness systems (air purification, circadian lighting)
board-formed concrete or plaster exterior with steel accents
luxury.renderGuidance: Render glass walls as genuinely transparent with real view content behind them (city grid, canyon, or ocean per the specific hillside); keep ceiling height doubled in the great room; maintain the monastic stone-and-wood palette without adding ornamentation; grounds should feel engineered/architectural, not landscaped in a traditional sense.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve the double-height great room and floor-to-ceiling glazing; keep the natural stone-and-wood material palette (travertine/limestone, white oak, blackened steel) rather than introducing color or heavy ornamentation; maintain minimal sightlines and disappearing glass walls.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the cantilevered hillside massing, flat/low-slope roofline, and view-facing glass wall; keep the linear infinity pool and its cantilever toward the view; do not add pitched roofs, shutters, or traditional detailing.

avoid: Adding pitched roofs or traditional ornament to a contemporary glass box
Rendering opaque or reflective 'fake' glass — the view must read through
Shrinking the double-height great room to a standard ceiling
Cluttering the minimal material palette with patterned finishes

sources.title: Inside A $63 Million Architectural Gem In LA's Famed Bird Streets (Nightingale, Woods and Dangaran)
Hollywood Hills Home Tour | California Contemporary
Touring a $48,000,000 LA Hillside Mansion with the BEST VIEWS OF LOS ANGELES
Touring a $23,995,000 Hillside Mansion with Views of Los Angeles

archetypes.name: Encino / Tarzana South-of-the-Boulevard Gated New Build
neighborhoods: Sherman Oaks (south of Ventura, gated pockets)
signature: Gated, hedge-walled frontage with a paver motor court and porte-cochere
Transitional-Mediterranean or modern-farmhouse-hybrid massing — smooth stucco/stone mix with large black or bronze windows
Resort backyard: infinity-edge or negative-edge pool, swim-up bar or pool house, sport court, sometimes a putting green
Flat, generously sized lot (Valley advantage vs. hillside lots elsewhere) allowing single-level living wings
Grand double-door entry, often glass-and-iron or an oversized wood pivot
interiorCharacter: 10-13 ft ceilings, open-concept great room with disappearing glass walls to the yard
Large center-island kitchen plus a full scullery/catering kitchen
Home theater, wine room, gym, and often a full guest/in-law wing
Primary suite with dual walk-in closets, freestanding tub, walk-through shower
Wide-plank engineered or white oak flooring, linear fireplaces
Smart-home integration (lighting, shades, AV) as a default expectation

typicalFinishes: Large-format porcelain slab or natural stone kitchen islands with waterfall edges
European white oak wide-plank flooring
La Cantina/Fleetwood glass wall systems opening to the yard
panel-ready Sub-Zero/Wolf suites, oversized pantry/scullery
matte-black or brushed-bronze door and plumbing hardware
smooth stucco with stone or wood accent cladding at the entry
resort pool decking in large-format pavers or travertine
luxury.renderGuidance: Render the flat-lot resort-backyard scale accurately (pool + pool house + sport court all fitting comfortably); keep the transitional material mix (smooth stucco + stone/wood accent + large black windows) rather than pushing fully traditional or fully industrial-modern; interiors should read new and crisp, not legacy-estate patinaed.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve the open-concept great room scale and 10+ ft ceiling height; maintain the transitional material mix of stone/quartz islands, white oak flooring, and matte-black hardware; keep glass-wall disappearing systems to the backyard intact.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the gated flat-lot motor court, smooth stucco/stone massing, and resort backyard proportions (pool, pool house, sport court); keep large-format black/bronze window framing; do not compress the flat-lot yard into a hillside-constrained footprint.

avoid: Shrinking the flat-lot resort backyard into a cramped hillside layout
Rendering builder-grade tract-home stucco detailing — this tier expects stone/wood accenting
Losing the large-format black window grid in exterior renders

sources.title: Touring an IMPRESSIVE Luxury Mansion in Encino!
This Newly-Built Encino Home Went Viral for All the Right Reasons ($8.9M, 7bd/8ba, 9,081 sqft gated estate)
Inside a $14,000,000 Hillside Modern Mansion with a Canyon Backyard (Tarzana)

archetypes.name: Manhattan Beach Coastal Modern (Sand/Hill Section)
neighborhoods: Manhattan Beach Sand Section (walk streets, The Strand)
signature: Narrow urban-infill lot (typically 30x90) requiring vertical stacking across 3-4 levels
Vertical wood-and-metal-trimmed facade with large Fleetwood glass openings
Bookend balconies/decks capturing ocean-to-Catalina or Malibu-to-Palos-Verdes view corridors
Residential elevator connecting floors, floating open-riser staircase as a centerpiece
Roof deck as primary outdoor living space (small/no yard at grade)
Two-to-three car garage at street level, living floors stacked above
interiorCharacter: Great room on the top living floor for maximum ocean view (kitchen/dining/living combined)
Wide-plank French oak flooring throughout
Floating built-ins, back-lit cabinetry and mirrors
Kitchen island in stone (quartzite/quartz) with waterfall edge, butterfly-cut walnut cabinetry
Stacked-stone or marble fireplace as a vertical feature wall
Primary suite often on its own floor with a private deck

typicalFinishes: Engineered white oak flooring, painted shaker cabinetry
quartz countertops, standard Fleetwood or comparable slider (not full-wall)
tile bath surrounds, standard plumbing fixture lines
composite deck at roof level, glass-panel railing
nice.renderGuidance: Keep the vertical massing and stacked-floor logic; coastal-fresh but a simpler material palette; roof deck remains but with lighter-touch furnishing.

typicalFinishes: Wide-plank French oak flooring throughout all levels
butterfly-cut or espresso walnut cabinetry with back-lit elements
Cambria/quartzite waterfall kitchen island, stingray-textured stone in the primary bath
Dornbracht/Watermark/Kallista fixtures, custom pendant lighting
Fleetwood full-height glass walls and bookend view balconies
residential elevator, floating staircase, vertically stacked stone fireplace
luxury.renderGuidance: Render the vertical, view-stacked massing accurately — this is not a sprawling single-level estate; keep glass openings large and framed in dark metal; ocean/Catalina view content should be visible and specific to the Sand Section vantage; the roof deck should read as the primary entertaining space.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve the vertical stacked-floor layout and the top-floor great room oriented to the view; keep wide-plank French oak flooring and dark-framed glass openings; maintain a coastal-contemporary palette (warm wood + stone, not a white-washed beach-cottage cliche).
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the narrow-lot vertical massing, dark window framing, and roof-deck-as-yard configuration; keep bookend balconies oriented toward the view corridor; do not add a ground-level yard this lot type doesn't have.

avoid: Flattening the vertical multi-story massing into a sprawling single-story estate
Inventing a large ground-level backyard on a Sand Section walk-street lot
Defaulting to an all-white beach-cottage look — luxury tier here is warm wood + stone contemporary

sources.title: Manhattan Beach Modern Home at 235 4th Street ($7,399,000, Fleetwood doors, French oak, walnut cabinetry)
212 Manhattan Avenue — 2026 Modern California Coastal (Ararat Developers/Lane Design + Build/CMS Interior Design)
Touring a $32.5 Million Oceanfront California Modern Mansion
Inside Kendrick Lamar's $9.7 Million Manhattan Beach Mansion

archetypes.name: San Marino / La Cañada Traditional Estate
archetypes.eras: 1910s-1940s Golden Age Colonial/Spanish/Tudor revival; 1980s-2000s traditional spec estates
signature: Georgian Colonial, Spanish Colonial Revival, or Tudor massing on 0.5-2+ acre flat lots
Formal symmetrical facade, brick or smooth stucco with clay tile or slate roofing
Motor court with circular drive, six-car garage or carriage house common on legacy estates
Mature specimen landscaping — oaks, camellias, formal boxwood gardens (Huntington-adjacent horticultural tradition)
Tennis court and/or pool as a separate, formally landscaped garden room rather than an integrated resort deck
interiorCharacter: Formal foyer and center-hall plan with symmetrical room flanking
10-12+ ft ceilings on the main level with plaster crown and picture-rail detail
Library/study with built-in bookcases and a fireplace
Formal dining room distinct from a separate breakfast room
Updated kitchens blend period-appropriate cabinetry with modern function (still separate from the great-room open-concept trend)
Primary suite as a distinct wing, often with a sitting room

typicalFinishes: Restored or replicated period millwork, plaster crown and ceiling medallions
book-matched marble in renovated baths paired with vintage-inspired tile
refinished or new white-oak/quarter-sawn-oak flooring, herringbone or basketweave in formal rooms
custom painted or stained wood cabinetry (not high-gloss modern slab) in updated kitchens
brass or aged-bronze period-appropriate hardware and lighting
clay tile or slate roof restoration, limestone or cast-stone exterior trim
luxury.renderGuidance: Renders must preserve period architectural authenticity — symmetry, roofline, millwork profiles — even when modernizing kitchen/bath function; updated spaces should read as sensitive renovations within a historic shell, not a contemporary insert; landscaping should read mature and formally structured (parterre gardens, allées), never new-build sod.

renderClauses.interior: Preserve the period architectural detailing (plaster crown, picture rail, center-hall symmetry) and ceiling height; when updating kitchen/bath finishes, keep them sympathetic to the era (painted/stained wood cabinetry, period-appropriate hardware) rather than inserting a stark modern-minimalist kitchen.
renderClauses.exterior: Preserve the symmetrical historic facade, roofline material (clay tile/slate), and mature formal landscaping; do not modernize the massing or strip period ornament; motor court and garage scale should remain estate-appropriate.

avoid: Inserting a stark contemporary slab-front kitchen into a Georgian/Spanish Colonial Revival shell
Stripping plaster crown molding or picture-rail detail in interior renders
Replacing mature formal gardens with sparse modern landscaping
Altering the symmetrical historic facade or roofline
